item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wow thats pretty cool +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did a read of this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bruh moment. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This -J was very fun to read and he was very fun to translate.

+1.

item.title: Heh
item.content:encoded:
Tentative +1 because I liked the examples.

I don't like that you invented a Humanity Preservation Association with no further explanation, and the last sentence is completely out of left field, adding exactly nothing of value. If you expanded this it could be better, but the description at least had me sold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I lost it at "SCP-3000 was killed and sold as sushi."

Great job! xD

item.title: Sides=Obliterated
item.content:encoded:
OK. I'm just going to post some of my favourite lines and let them speak for themselves.

SCP-3000 was killed and sold as sushi.

SCP-106's containment chamber was replaced with a cardboard box. Signs spelling: "DONT GET OUT YA BASTARD" were plastered on each side of its interior.

Automatic doors were replaced with wooden doors. All personnel are now required to make mandatory "whoosh" noises whenever one is opened.

Foundation members are to beg the Humanity Preservation Association for further funding through mail, e-mails, real life harassment, phone calls, telegrams, and anomalous voodoo dolls.

So yeah. I think that makes it pretty clear why I upvoted this…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There probably is untapped potential here, but I laughed quietly to myself several times (what do you want? I'm at work), so I'm upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know if this is the result of the edits or not, but, like…

You invented a brand new fictional agency (which apparently pays for the whole Foundation?) and a fictional event to explain severe budget cuts… and then just listed mildly-to-moderately amusing things that happened due to budget cuts.

Not only do those things beg explanation, they're wasted opportunities for humor. The rest just isn't funny enough. At least the other two were commenting on something, this just feels like b-material.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are most electrons now hamburgers, or just a few? Because that could be either disastrous or delicious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought I was going to like this, but the ending felt a bit lolrandom rather than funny, I however loved the budget containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So dumb, so of course I laughed a lot.

SCP-106's containment chamber was replaced with a cardboard box. Signs spelling: "DONT GET OUT YA BASTARD" were plastered on each side of its interior.

This in particular got me. XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You can't please everyone, my dear Spooky

Of course not. It's not like he's coffee.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can't please everyone, my dear Spooky :p just go with what feels right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ha ha this is so Terry Pratchett! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this would have been funnier if written from the in-universe perspective of someone facing budget cuts. Sort of in the same vein as SCP-1150-J. This premise is amusing but it doesn't have a punchline or really a joke other than "isn't this kind of amusing?" Which it is, but that's not enough to carry the article. It ultimately needs some kind of punch, the political angle suggested above could be good if you just made it about budget cuts in general. That's not necessarily going to be seen as a partisan thing since any kind of political party in power could cut a budget. Overall, tl;dr, this needs a punchline or some kind of next step to fully realize its potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Had a sensible chuckle, just like in drafts. +1

I like the short and to-the-point format.

What does Stan From Accounting have to say about these budget cuts?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, especially because mainlist articles are often up for years; if you make it specific to current events, all you're doing is ensuring that the article becomes dated within a few months.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked it earlier in the drafts, like it now. +1

I understand why people may want more from it, but I'm of the opinion that bogging this down in too many one-liners might be of detriment to the article. Maybe I'm wrong there (because I am in the minority) but I furthermore don't think every -J article needs to be laugh-out-loud funny. This is very amusing and got a giggle out of me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, fair enough. I added governmental bureaucracy into the mix.

Nevermind, Weryllium opposes this.

Okay, I found a middle ground for everyone. I made it more subtle.

I'm trying to please everyone but it's not working.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eugh. Not everything has to mirror current events, and I personally think it'd be funnier if the core concept was played to the extreme than trying to shoehorn in a completely different angle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In addition to the calls for more content, I think this could stand out a bit, conceptually, if it took on a more political bent. What with the nigh-parodical proposed cuts to essential government services and programs as of late (in the USA), I had actually expected this to openly parody that, and was kind of disappointed when they didn't. It wouldn't be necessary to make names, just put Foundation spins on stuff that's happened and things people have said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's amusing and cute, but it didn't even warrant a chuckle. No-vote from me, especially since there's just not enough here. It needs more meat: more on the budget cuts, auctions, comical ineptness with remaining funds, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I smiled a little, but I never really laughed. Like the others have said, it's amusing, but it never crossed over into funny.

The similarities to 231-J and 1150-J are part of the problem, I think- right off the bat you're pitting yourself against both Russian gallows humor and DaveYouFool on his A-game. (I'm also reminded of the Cimmerian-Kaktus Proposal for 001-J.) Those together set a high bar to match. It's not impossible, but it'll take a lot of polishing to get there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm in the same boat; it's a cool concept and the execution isn't bad, but it's not great either. I'd love to see some refinement to the humor. No-vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid my earlier criticisms remain; there just isn't enough of it to really grab me. It needs something extra.

However, I do like the concept a lot, so I'm no-voting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to DesperadoZod, Mortos, Chubert, Tufto, Flawed, bittermixin, Orionbasher, Shio, mlister, Nekromykon and many others who read and gave this feedback when it was a draft.

Wow, this is pretty similar to SCP-231-J
hide block

Although I can see the similarities between the two articles, they have different objectives. SCP-231-J is mainly centered around parodying 231 and showing how a small loan can fuck up the Foundation real badly. 8851-J is centered around parodying containment procedures and showing what happens when the Foundation has no money.

Wow, this is pretty similar to SCP-1150-J
hide block

Well kinda, but again, the focus is different. Quoting Gene R: "It's a joke on bureaucracy, poor management, irresponsibility and corruption in general." It's a big "fuck you" from the Russian site to companies which mistreat them after the collapse of the USSR. 8851-J is centered around the Foundation and other organizations having no money to begin with.
